title: pi-context update result does not make partial application legible — a blocked run can read as a no-op while config.json was mutated
description: update applies per-component (a blocked schema rolls back only itself; the additive config-registry propagation writes regardless — affirmed by DEC-0018), but the result does not make that partiality legible. A live run that returns a schema under `blocked` still mutated config.json with the catalog-new registry vocabulary (relation_types/block_kinds/invariants/lenses), yet nothing in the result ties the two together: `blocked` and `registryAdditions` are independent channels, so a caller reading `blocked` can reasonably conclude nothing was applied while config.json in fact changed. General class: update's result reporting does not surface its per-component partial application across the independent seams (schema-loop outcomes + registry propagation); the registry-write-while-a-schema-blocked case is the triggering instance.

evidence.reference: post-loop registry propagation computes registryAdditions and calls writeConfig when !dryRun, ungated on schema-loop outcome — runs even when a sibling schema is in result.blocked
the schema-loop result buckets (resynced/migrated/blocked) are reported independently of registryAdditions; no field ties a blocked outcome to the config mutation that still occurred
R-0012 — live reproduction: conventions blocked, registryAdditions (incl. item_derived_from_item) written to config.json in the same run

impact: A user or agent reading a `blocked` update result can assume the substrate was left unchanged and skip reconciling a config.json that did change — silent partial application. The risk is legibility, not data loss (the additive write is safe); it undermines trust in the result as a faithful account of what update did.
proposed_resolution: To be determined exactly from live codebase.
